title: The trigger of someone else crying or being in pain
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
I can't handle other people's pain. Especially crying.

You're at a friend's house. They start talking about something hard. Their voice cracks. Their eyes fill. And you feel it. That tightness in your chest. That urge to fix it, stop it, make it go away. Or maybe you go numb. You disconnect. Your body goes still and your mind goes blank. Either way, you're not present anymore. You're somewhere else, trying to survive the sound of someone else's tears.

Here's the thing. This isn't a sign that you're a bad friend or a cold person. It's a sign that your nervous system has learned that crying equals danger. That someone else's pain is a threat to your own safety. And that's not your fault. That's your history talking.

I've been there. I still go there sometimes. Someone starts crying and my body goes into high alert. My shoulders hike up toward my ears. My breathing gets shallow. I start scanning for exits, for solutions, for anything that will make the tears stop. Because somewhere along the way, my nervous system learned that other people's pain is not safe. It's overwhelming. It's too much. And if I don't fix it, something bad will happen.

This is actually a very common response for people with complex trauma. You might not even realize you do it until someone points it out. But once you see it, you can't unsee it. And that's where the real work begins.

Why someone else's tears feel like a threat to you

Let's get into the science for a minute. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called neuroception. It's the way your nervous system scans for safety and danger without your conscious mind even knowing about it. Your neuroception is constantly reading the room. It's picking up on facial expressions, tone of voice, body language, even the pace of someone's breathing. And when it detects a signal that looks like distress, it makes a split-second decision: safe, dangerous, or life-threatening.

For most people, someone crying registers as a signal of distress. That's normal. But for someone with a trauma history, that signal can get amplified. Your nervous system doesn't just hear crying. It hears a siren. It hears a warning that something is very, very wrong and that you need to act now or you will be in danger too.

Think about it. When you were a kid, if your caregiver was in distress, what happened? Maybe they ignored you. Maybe they got angry. Maybe they fell apart and you had to take care of them. Maybe you learned that their pain meant your needs would not be met. That their tears were a sign that you were about to be abandoned, hurt, or overwhelmed. And your nervous system made a note: crying equals threat.

That note is still playing in your body today. Even if you're an adult. Even if you're safe. Even if the person crying is just a friend who needs to vent for five minutes. Your body doesn't know the difference. It's reacting to an old map of the world, not the one you're actually living in.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about this in terms of unfinished survival responses. Your body wants to do something. Fight, flight, freeze, or fawn. It wants to act. But in a social situation, you can't run away or punch someone for crying. So you freeze. Or you fawn. You try to soothe them, fix them, make them stop. Not because you're a good person. But because your nervous system is trying to survive.

And that's the hard truth. Your urge to fix someone's pain is not always compassion. Sometimes it's self-protection. You need them to stop crying so you can feel safe again. And that's okay to admit. That's honest. That's where the real healing starts.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). It's not a cure, but it can help take the edge off that hypervigilant state. Your body uses magnesium to regulate the nervous system, and trauma survivors are often deficient. It's one small piece of the puzzle, but it can make a difference.

The freeze response and the disappearing self

There's another version of this trigger that looks different but feels just as awful. You don't get activated. You don't feel the urge to fix or flee. Instead, you disappear. Someone starts crying and you just go blank. You feel nothing. You might even feel annoyed or impatient. You wonder why they can't just pull themselves together. You disconnect from your own body and from theirs.

This is the freeze response. And it's just as much a trauma reaction as the panic or the fixing urge. Your nervous system has learned that the only way to survive overwhelming emotion is to shut it off completely. You go numb because feeling anything would be too much. You can't afford to feel their pain because you can barely handle your own.

I've been on both sides of this. There have been times when someone cried and I felt this cold wall go up inside me. Like I was watching a movie. Like their tears had nothing to do with me. And then later, alone, I would feel the guilt. The shame. The question: What's wrong with me? Why can't I just be there for them?

Here's what I've learned. Nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it learned to do to keep you alive. The problem is that the strategy that kept you safe as a child is now keeping you isolated as an adult. The wall that protected you is now a prison.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist and author, talks about this in terms of parts of the self. There's a part of you that learned to shut down when emotions got too big. That part is not bad. It's not broken. It's trying to protect you. But it's stuck in an old job. It doesn't realize that you're not a child anymore. That you have resources now. That you can handle someone's tears without being destroyed.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It's a dense read, but it offers a map for understanding why you react the way you do. It helped me see my own freeze response not as a character flaw but as a survival strategy that just needs updating.

Learning to stay in the room with someone else's pain

So what do you do with all of this? How do you actually change the way you respond when someone cries?

The first step is not to try to stop your reaction. The first step is to notice it. To become curious about it. To say to yourself, Oh, there it is. That tightness in my chest. That urge to fix. That blankness. That's my nervous system doing its old job. It's trying to protect me. Thank you, nervous system. I see you. But I'm okay right now.

That might sound silly. But it works. Because the moment you name the reaction, you create a tiny bit of space between you and it. You're no longer just the reaction. You're the one observing the reaction. And that's a completely different neural pathway.

You can also use your breath. Not to calm down. Not to make the feeling go away. But to stay present. To let your body know that you are not in danger. That you can breathe slowly even while someone else is crying. That their tears do not mean you are about to die.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It gives your breath a physical anchor. You breathe in, and then you slow the exhale through the device. That extended exhale signals safety to your vagus nerve. It's not magic. But it's a tool you can use in the moment, or in practice, to retrain your nervous system over time.

Another thing that helps is to practice with small doses. You don't have to jump into the deep end. You can start by watching a sad movie and noticing what happens in your body. You can read a story about someone's pain and just let yourself feel it for thirty seconds before you close the tab. You can tell a friend, Hey, I'm working on being more present when people are upset. Can we practice? Can you just share something small and let me try to stay with you?

Honestly, this is some of the hardest work you'll ever do. It's not about getting it right. It's about showing up and failing and showing up again. It's about learning that you can survive someone else's tears. That you don't have to fix them. That sometimes the most healing thing you can do is just sit there, in the discomfort, and let them be human.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk walks through how trauma lives in the body and how we can begin to release it. It's a classic for a reason. It helped me understand that my reactions weren't just in my head. They were in my cells. And that understanding made it easier to be compassionate with myself.

The hard truth about fixing

Here's something I don't hear people say enough. Sometimes when you try to fix someone's pain, you are actually abandoning them. You're leaving their emotional reality to go find a solution. You're telling them, without words, that their feelings are too much for you to hold. That they need to stop feeling so you can feel better.

That's a hard thing to hear. I know. Because I've done it. I've been the fixer. I've been the one who can't sit still in someone else's grief. And I've had to learn that my fixing was not helping. It was hurting. It was telling the people I loved that their pain was a problem to be solved, not a reality to be witnessed.

But here's the other side. You can't just decide to stop fixing. You have to do the work underneath it. You have to find out why their pain feels like a threat. You have to heal the part of you that learned that tears are dangerous. And that takes time. That takes patience. That takes a lot of uncomfortable moments where you fail and fail and fail again.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you feel a tightening when I said that? A resistance? A desire to argue or shut down? That's okay. That's the work showing up. That's your nervous system saying, I don't want to feel this. And that's exactly where we need to start.
